title: Module 2 quiz: Mutations with Server Actions

choices.text: Most REST/tRPC mutation endpoints. A Server Action is an async function with `'use server'` directive that runs on the server but is called from Client Components as if local. Next.js auto-generates the RPC plumbing; types flow through. Works with native HTML forms via `action={fn}` for progressive enhancement — forms work even without JavaScript. Keep REST endpoints for external consumers (mobile apps, webhooks); use Server Actions for app-internal mutations.

questions.explanation: Server Actions replace most REST mutation endpoints with type-safe co-located functions.

questions.prompt: Server Action creates a post and redirects to /posts. List shows stale data. Cause?

choices.text: Missing revalidation before the redirect. Call `revalidatePath('/posts')` (or `revalidateTag('posts')`) after the DB write and before `redirect()`. The /posts page was cached; without invalidation the redirected page serves stale data. Forgetting revalidation after a mutation is the most common Server Actions bug.

questions.explanation: Call revalidatePath/revalidateTag before redirect. Otherwise cache serves stale.

questions.prompt: Why validate server-side in addition to (or instead of) client-side?

choices.text: Server validation is authoritative — the security boundary. Malicious actors bypass client-side validation by calling actions directly. Server validation also works without JavaScript (progressive enhancement). Client-side validation is fine as a UX enhancement giving instant feedback without a round-trip, but the server validates on submit regardless. Use the same Zod schema in both for consistency. The server is the truth; the client is the polish.

questions.explanation: Server is the security boundary; client validation is UX polish.

questions.prompt: Building a like button — toggling should feel instant but write to the DB. Pattern?

choices.text: Use `useOptimistic` to update the UI immediately before the server confirms. Click handler calls `addOptimistic(newValue)` to flip the local state, then awaits the Server Action. The button shows the new state instantly; on success the real data syncs; on failure React reverts. Use for high-frequency low-risk mutations (likes, toggles, adds). Don't use for complex multi-step or irreversible operations where rollback would confuse.

questions.explanation: useOptimistic for instant UI; auto-reverts on failure.

questions.prompt: Where should real auth checks happen?

choices.text: Middleware does cheap checks (cookie exists? JWT valid? not expired?) to redirect early; layouts/Server Actions do the full check (load user, check status, RBAC). Server Actions ALWAYS verify on their own — never trust UI-level gating. An attacker can call any action directly via browser devtools or curl regardless of what UI you hide; only the server's `if (!session) throw` is real security. Hide buttons for UX; verify on server for safety.

questions.explanation: Middleware (cheap) + Layout/Action (deep). Server is the security boundary.
